experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Today in the Word website visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Today in the Word’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Dear Reader.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for one and a half weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learning
The primary takeaway from this experiment is that adding a premium image to the Today in the Word website did not increase engagement or downstream donor metrics. Instead, the treatment featuring the premium image experienced a statistically valid 47% decrease in clicks compared to the control.
This decline extended to giving behavior. The control generated six gifts through the Dear Reader, while the treatment generated none—resulting in a 100% decrease in gifts at a 98% level of confidence.
Importantly, this outcome was consistent across devices. Clicks declined on mobile by 43% (valid), on desktop by 81% (valid), and on tablet by 21% (27% LOC). While desktop users responded most negatively to the image, the pattern indicates that users on all devices were less likely to click the CTA when the image was present.
Implications
These results suggest that the premium image may have distracted from the core message or failed to reinforce the value of the offer for this audience. Rather than increasing motivation, the visual element appears to have introduced friction at a critical decision point.
Next Steps
Future experimentation should approach visual elements with caution and intentionality. Continued testing of alternative imagery—or the removal of images altogether—will help identify what best supports clarity, engagement, and conversion. Ongoing analysis of engagement and donation data will remain essential to guiding optimizations that drive meaningful donor response.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a high urgency campaign impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a monthly first ask for Fall Share giving page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving. because  more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2025 - 09/26/2025
Each year, Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign to inspire generosity among listeners.
This year, we made a strategic shift: instead of prioritizing one-time gifts, we focused on growing Moody’s recurring donor file. To accomplish this, we tested a monthly-first ask during the Fall Share campaign.
The goal was to build their recurring file while minimizing the negative impact on overall conversion on the giving page.
The test ran for 11 days, with traffic evenly split between each variant.
Note: The majority of participants came from known audiences. While a separate non-donor–specific donation page was available, many of the web assets directed to this general campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a monthly-first ask for Fall Share donation page visitors significantly increased recurring giving. The treatment group experienced a 168.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence in the results.
Furthermore, with this large increase in recurring giving, we wanted to be sure it wasn't at the expense of overall transactions. After further analysis, we discovered the treatment was resulting in a 5.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 42% level of confidence. Based on these results, the value of the more recurring donors outweighs the slight (and invalid) decrease in overall giving.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize the monthly-first ask strategy, as it proved to be highly effective in increasing recurring donor conversions without significantly reducing overall transactions. This approach capitalizes on the convenience and appeal of monthly giving for donors, leading to a higher likelihood of acceptance of the pre-set option and a perception of monthly giving as both the recommended and more manageable choice. By consistently incorporating this strategy in future campaigns and experiments, we can continue to grow our recurring donor file and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a direct ask vs survey during a high urgency campaign lower third impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the Listener Survey treatment variant for Moody Radio website visitors will increase donor conversion. By first inviting visitors to share their feedback, the survey fosters a sense of being valued and deepens their emotional connection with Moody Radio, making them more likely to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2025 - 09/16/2025
During Moody Radio’s 2025 Fall Share campaign, we tested two approaches: a direct ask to give toward Fall Share and an invitation to complete the Listener Survey.
Our goal was to learn whether Moody Radio’s audience responds more strongly to straightforward giving language, or if they prefer first engaging through a survey before being invited to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the direct ask approach to give toward Fall Share outperformed the invitation to complete the Listener Survey in terms of donor conversion. Despite hypothesizing that the survey would increase engagement and emotional connection, leading to more donations, the results showed a significant decrease in transactions for the survey variant.

In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully consider the preferences and behaviors of Moody Radio's audience. While exploring different approaches to engage donors is valuable, the results indicate that a straightforward giving ask may be more effective in driving donations during campaigns.

Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue testing and refining messaging and calls to action to align with audience preferences. Additionally, gathering feedback from donors and website visitors through surveys or interviews can provide insights into their motivations and preferences, helping to inform future experimentation and fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How the addition of the recurring interrupter on acquisition pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the recurring interrupter for Moody Radio acquisition giving page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving without negatively impacting overall transactions because  it invites donors to commit to a smaller gift today that multiplies into greater long-term impact, we can grow recurring revenue without reducing immediate generosity. because   it invites donors to commit to a smaller gift today that multiplies into greater long-term impact, we can grow recurring revenue without reducing immediate generosity.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/08/2025
To strengthen Moody’s monthly partner program, we introduced a recurring gift interrupter on Moody Radio’s acquisition giving pages.
This interrupter was displayed to donors initiating a one-time gift under $250, with a dynamic ask based on their original selection:
$200–$250 gift → Suggested $25 monthly commitment
$100–$199 gift → Suggested $15 monthly commitment
Under $100 gift → Suggested $10 monthly commitment
The primary goal was to increase the number of Moody Radio’s monthly partners—building sustainable revenue—while ensuring there was no negative impact on overall one-time giving transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key findings from this experiment demonstrate that adding a recurring gift interrupter to Moody Radio’s acquisition giving pages led to a 217.7% increase in monthly donations with a 98.3% level of statistical confidence. This shows that inviting donors—who initially intended to give a one-time gift under $250—to consider a smaller monthly commitment successfully grew the number of monthly partners without reducing one-time giving.
Importantly, overall transactions were not negatively affected. In fact, we observed a 1.7% increase in total transactions (18.8% LOC), providing further evidence that the interrupter did not harm conversion rates on the giving page.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue implementing similar strategies that encourage donors to consider converting their one-time gifts into recurring monthly commitments. This can be achieved by carefully tailoring the suggested monthly amounts based on the original one-time gift selection, as demonstrated in this experiment. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further analyze donor behavior and preferences to continue optimizing the recurring gift interrupter for maximum effectiveness. By leveraging the success of this experiment and continuously testing and refining our approaches, we can further grow Moody Radio’s monthly partner program and enhance sustainable revenue for the organization.

experiments.title: How eliminating the form frame and simplifying opt-in language on acquisition landing pages impacts email opt ins

rows.name: Remove Form Frame + Condensed Opt-In language/Preselect

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating the form frame and simplifying the opt-in copy for Today in the Word acquisition landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email optins because it reduces the friction and decision fatigue in the form submission process allowing for a more streamlined approach for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 07/17/2025
In an effort to increase email opt-ins on Today in the Word’s acquisition funnels, we wanted to try simplifying the messaging around opting in as well as cleaning up the form around it.
The treatment removed the form frame as well as condensed down the language around the checkbox to opt in.
Control: Stay grounded in your faith through God’s Word! Get our Daily Devotional and Bible-centered resources from Today in the Word! Sign Me Up!
Treatment: “I want to stay grounded in faith through God’s Word! Send me the Daily Devotional.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the opt-in copy and removing the form frame on the Today in the Word acquisition landing page led to a significant decrease in opt-ins for all traffic, with a 46.8% decrease at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence. This indicates that the treatment did not resonate well with visitors and may have caused confusion or hesitation in opting in for Bible-centric content and resources from Moody Radio.
Furthermore, the experiment also showed a considerable decrease in One-Time Donations, Transactions, and Revenue, with decreases ranging from 758% to 764% at statistical confidence levels of 96% to 100%. This suggests that the changes made to the opt-in process not only impacted the opt-ins but also other key metrics related to donations and transactions.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of simplifying messaging and form layouts on user behavior and conversion rates. While streamlining the opt-in process may work in some cases, it is crucial to test and validate these changes thoroughly before implementation. Additionally, it is essential to prioritize user experience and ensure that any modifications to the acquisition funnels are user-friendly and clear to visitors to avoid any potential drop in engagement or conversions.

experiments.title: How does showing donors that their gift reaches all three Moody Bible Institute ministries influence conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the impact of a gift on all three MBI ministries for Moody Radio’s Book of Signs premium donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because by clearly demonstrating that their contribution reaches beyond a single ministry, we highlight the added value of their gift and inspire giving by showing it can go further and impact more lives.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2025 - 08/13/2025
Moody Bible Institute encompasses three distinct ministries, but their brands are often marketed separately. We wanted to test whether donors would see greater value in giving to one ministry while having an impact across all three. To explore this, we ran a test within Moody Radio’s Book of Signs premium ask funnel to measure donor response.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including the impact of a gift on all three Moody Bible Institute ministries for Moody Radio's Book of Signs premium donation page visitors led to a 22.4% decrease in revenue for all traffic. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% level of confidence, indicating a clear difference between the control and treatment groups.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and framing of donation appeals. While the intention was to show the donors that their gift could have a wider impact across all three ministries, it inadvertently resulted in a decrease in revenue.
Moving forward, it may be beneficial to conduct more qualitative research to understand donor preferences and motivations before implementing changes to donation pages. Additionally, A/B testing with smaller sample sizes or conducting pilot studies can help in gauging the potential impact of changes before a full rollout. It's crucial to continually test and iterate on donation strategies to find the most effective approaches for engaging donors and driving revenue for Moody Radio and the Moody Bible Institute as a whole.

experiments.title: How does a premium focused vs impact-focused donation page impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impact-focused copy for The Pursuit of God donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates an emotional connection to Moody Bible Institute’s mission before presenting the premium offer, thereby increasing the perceived value of that offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2025 - 08/13/2025
The Moody Bible Institute recently started running acquisition ads. For one of their first offers, The Pursuit of God, we offered a free ebook of the resource but the donation page offered the physical copy with a gift of any amount.
We wanted to learn more about the motivations of EDU donors, so we tested framing the donation page copy around the premium vs around the impact of the gift.
We believe the treatment will outperform the control because it opens with a student-centered impact story that builds emotional connection and urgency before presenting the premium offer. This impact-first approach is likely to increase perceived value and inspire more donors to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment conducted for the donation page for The Pursuit of God resource is that the control, which focused on the premium offer, outperformed the treatment, which focused on the impact of the gift. The treatment, despite its impact-focused approach, resulted in a significant 53.9% decrease in revenue for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
From this experiment, we have learned that, in this specific case, donors were more responsive to the premium offer than they were to the impact-focused copy. In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing different messaging and approaches to understand donor motivations better. It is essential to tailor the messaging and offers based on the audience and context to maximize donation revenue.
Overall, this experiment emphasizes the importance of testing and understanding donor preferences, as well as the need to constantly iterate and refine strategies based on data and results.

experiments.title: Will positioning a premium offer as monthly first increase recurring transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that directly asking for a monthly gift in the Killing Sin Premium Donation Page copy, combined with a donation form that defaults to monthly giving, will increase the number of recurring gifts by reducing decision ambiguity, framing monthly giving as the expected norm, and reinforcing the value of sustained impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2025 - 08/13/2025
Moody has several Premium offers that their audiences seems to respond to really well. For this test we wanted to see if we can position the donation page on one of their premiums to be monthly focused copy and defaulting the form to monthly, making it the standard choice for users who land on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Directly asking for a monthly gift in the Killing Sin Premium Donation Page copy, combined with a donation form that defaults to monthly giving, resulted in a significant decrease in one-time donations by 42.2%. While we did see an increase in recurring transactions, the sample size is small for recurring on both. While we would prefer recurring donors because their LTV is so much greater, the tradeoff for the loss of one time donors is not worth it here.
In conclusion, while the hypothesis did not yield the expected results in this experiment, the findings provide valuable insights that can be used to refine future fundraising strategies and optimize donation pages for better performance.

experiments.title: How will donations be impacted if we add a step in the giving process?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading users to the landing page instead of the donation page for the 48 Hours of Impact hero image link will achieve less transactions because we will be adding more friction for the user to get to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/01/2025
Moody Radio is running a 48 Hours of Impact high urgency campaign. Leading up to the event, we wanted to test and see how leading users from the homepage to the landing page instead of the giving page would impact overall transactions.
To test this on the homepage we updated the 48 Hours of Impact Hero Image to go to the landing page instead of the donation page. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that leading users from the homepage to the landing page instead of the donation page for the 48 Hours of Impact hero image link resulted in a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group saw a 90.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence of 99.6%.

This suggests that adding an extra step or friction for users to get to the donation page can deter them from completing the donation process. In future experimentation, it is important to prioritize a seamless and direct path for users to make donations. It is also valuable to consider the user experience and minimize any unnecessary steps that could potentially hinder donation conversions.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of streamlining the donation process and making it as easy and straightforward as possible for users to support the cause.

experiments.title: How including a gift array in a high urgency campaign sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift array buttons to a lower third for Today in the Word homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/10/2025
During Today in the Word’s FYE Campaign, we tested adding gift array buttons into the lower third vs a one-button ask in a lower third. For this test, we tracked CTA clicks on both the control and treatment to measure how effective each one of these web assets persuaded someone to navigate to the donation page, as well as transaction and revenue that came directly from these assets.
This test ran for 8 days and the treatment was shown to 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding gift array buttons to a lower third for Today in the Word homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment resulted in a 172.0% increase in Transactions (src=tw_fye25_footer*) for All Traffic with a 98.2% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment was successful in persuading users to navigate to the donation page and complete a transaction. Based on these results, we should be using a lower third with a gift array in it for future lower thirds for Today in the Word.
We also tracked clicks on both the control and treatment lower thirds and the treatment saw a 51.4% lift in clicks ( 97.1% LOC) compared to the control. These results indicate that the treatment not only encouraged more people to navigate to the donation page, but also resulted in an increase in transcations.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of providing users with the opportunity to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page. This can streamline the giving process for users and potentially lead to higher conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different variations of donation prompts and CTAs to optimize donor conversion metrics further.

experiments.title: TW – Will changing the donation page to be focused on the donor impact affect conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the reader’s personal connection to the devotional and the fact that it is entirely donor-funded for individuals landing on the donation page will achieve more donations because it makes the impact of their gift feel immediate, essential, and personally meaningful—motivating them to take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/25/2025
Today in the Word is a ministry that offers daily devotionals. We have an acquisition funnel running all the time to get names into their daily email devotional.
For this test, we wanted to see if changing the copy on the donation page for this acquisition funnel would increase the number of gifts.
The control version focuses on the organizational legacy and mission of Moody Bible Institute, emphasizing its long history and continued commitment to spreading the Gospel. The tone is formal, mission-driven, and emphasizes how the gift will be used for broad gospel impact.
The treatment version focuses more directly on the devotional itself and the personal impact it has on readers. It highlights that the devotional is donor-supported, the number of people being reached daily, and positions the donor as a direct enabler of that spiritual impact. The tone is more personal, emotionally resonant, and focused on the reader’s role in expanding access to this devotional.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that emphasizing the reader's personal connection to the devotional and the fact that it is entirely donor-funded significantly increased donations and engagement. By highlighting the direct impact of their gift on expanding access to the devotional and making it feel immediate, essential, and personally meaningful, individuals were motivated to take action and donate.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize personalizing the donor experience and focusing on the individual's role in supporting the mission. By highlighting the immediate and tangible outcomes of their donation, we can continue to drive higher levels of engagement and donations. Additionally, we should consider testing different elements of personalization and emotional resonance to further optimize our acquisition funnels and maximize impact.

experiments.title: TW – What happens when we present an email sign up opportunity higher on the page?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the email CTA bigger and easier to fill out for the homepage will achieve more emails acquired because it’s more visible .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/25/2025
Today in the Word is a ministry that offers daily devotionals delivered right to your email.
When looking at the Today in the Word site, we noticed that the only sign up for receive emails was in the footer. There is a CTA right below the hero image, but it was very small and easily to miss. With the main goal of this site being to get email address, we wondered what would happen if we made that CTA bigger and easier to fill out right on the page.
This test only showed on the homepage for new visitors
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. **Visibility is crucial**: By making the email CTA bigger and easier to fill out on the homepage, there was a significant increase in email acquisitions. This indicates that visibility plays a key role in encouraging visitors to take action.
2. **Simplify the process**: Making it easier for users to sign up for emails directly on the homepage likely reduced friction and made it more convenient for them to engage with the content.
3. **Data-driven decisions**: The significant increase in email acquisitions and the high level of statistical confidence in the results emphasize the importance of data-driven decision-making in optimizing website performance.
Moving forward, these learnings can be applied in future experimentation by:
1. **Prioritizing visibility**: Ensuring that important CTAs or conversion points are prominently displayed on the website to attract the attention of visitors and encourage action.
2. **Continuing to simplify**: Streamlining the user experience by simplifying processes such as email sign-ups can lead to higher engagement and conversion rates.
3. **Testing and iterating**: Building on the success of this experiment, it's important to continue testing variations to further optimize the website for conversions and user engagement. Regular experiments and A/B testing can help in refining strategies based on user behavior and preferences.

experiments.title: How preselecting the gift frequency and gift amount on a giving form impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that preselecting and expanding the gift array for Today in the Word acquisition giving page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it will help reduce decision friction for donors by clearly presenting the preferred giving frequency and gift size within the acquisition funnel .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 08/24/2025
Moody Bible’s giving forms have historically been designed without pre-selected gift frequencies or amounts. While this has always been their standard setup, it differs from many other donation platforms where defaults are commonly used.
To test whether defaults could reduce decision friction and encourage giving, we introduced two pre-selections:
Gift frequency: defaulted to one-time
Gift amount: defaulted to the lowest option in the gift array ($25)
Our goal was to streamline the donor experience—minimizing effort and uncertainty—while also testing whether this change could increase overall conversion and potentially lift the average gift amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated that pre-selecting gift frequency and amount—combined with expanding the gift array options—produced a significant lift in donor conversion metrics.
The treatment group outperformed the control with a 454.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic (94.4% LOC) and a 1,015% increase in Revenue (100% LOC).
 	Notably, while the sample size was small, the treatment showed no negative impact on transactions or revenue, indicating a clear positive response from Today in the Word’s audience.
 	The average gift amount also increased by 116.7%, confirming our hypothesis that the treatment would encourage larger donations.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
The addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, there was an 8.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in terms of transactions.
 	Despite the decrease in transactions, there was a significant increase in revenue by 489% in the treatment group with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that although fewer people may have donated in the treatment group, those who did contribute gave more on average.
 	The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in transactions is 82.7%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered reliable or used for future decision-making.

experiments.keyLearnings:

Although the results did not reach a statistical level of confidence of 95%, the trend indicates that the control (current homepage format) performed better than the treatment (focus on premium).

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the potential impact of changing the format of the ask on different parts of the website. It is clear that continuity and consistency in messaging across different pages can play a crucial role in building trust and credibility with donors. Therefore, it may be beneficial to maintain a similar approach to the ask throughout the website to ensure a unified donor experience.

Further testing is recommended to validate these findings and to explore other factors that may influence donation conversions on content pages. Adjusting the format of the ask, testing different premiums, or even considering the timing of the ask in the donor journey could all be valuable areas for future experimentation to optimize donation conversions for the Spring Share campaign on Moody Radio's website.

Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the radical redesign of the landing page for ebook offers resulted in a 12.8% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic. This indicates that the changes made in the treatment version may have had a positive impact on attracting more email conversions.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to further validate these findings. By continuing to test different variations of the landing page, we can gather more data to determine if the radical redesign consistently leads to higher email conversions. Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze user feedback and behavior to understand why the treatment version performed better and make informed decisions for future optimizations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that simplifying the landing page and focusing on key elements such as the ebook image can potentially enhance the user experience and encourage more email sign-ups. This information can guide future strategies for improving email capture rates on landing pages for Moody Radio ministries.

experiments.title: Will a personalized dear reader perform better than a non personalized?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a personalized ask for the dear reader on the content page will achieve a higher donor conversion because the ask is more humanized and they can associate it with the editor that they see writing them everyday.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
In previous campaigns we’ve seen dear readers work remarkable well for TW. For this test we wanted to build off of our previous tests to find the best way to present a dear reader to their audience.
In this case, we wanted to see if having a personalized ask for the editor would improve conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that having a personalized ask for the editor in the content page did not result in an increase in donor conversion rates as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in donations compared to the control group.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze why the personalized ask did not resonate with the audience as expected. It may be beneficial to conduct further research to better understand the preferences and motivations of the audience before implementing personalized asks. Additionally, it is essential to always run experiments to statistically significant levels of confidence (95% or higher) to ensure the results are reliable.

Moving forward, it may be valuable to test different types of personalization or messaging to see what resonates best with donors. By learning from this experiment and making adjustments based on the findings, we can continue to optimize our strategies for increasing donor conversions in future campaigns.

experiments.title: Will a guided reading experience improve donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by arranging our giving page copy in a problem/solution format for the calendar year end campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donation page will be easier to read and understand the impact that the donor makes.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2024 - 12/27/2024
For our year-end campaign, we wanted to explore whether presenting the donation message in a problem/solution format would help donors feel a stronger connection to the impact of their calendar year-end gift. Specifically, we aimed to see if framing the need for support in terms of how donations directly provide a solution to more readers with the valuable materials and guidance offered by Today in the Word would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a guided reading experience to the donation page resulted in a 28.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was at 83.4%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should be tested further during another campaign or during a season without a campaign.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 95% to draw reliable conclusions. It might be worth revisiting the guided reading experience concept in future tests, possibly with adjustments or improvements, to see if it consistently leads to increased donor conversions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to continue experimenting with different elements on the donation page to optimize for the highest conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Lower Third Ad vs a Sticky Bar Perform Better on the Today in the Word Homepage During Calendar Year End?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in a lower third ad for calendar year end on the homepage will achieve greater donor conversion because we’re introducing a novel web element that contains more gift options to interact with during critical giving days of the year.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2024 - 01/01/2025
During campaigns for Today in the Word, we often use a sticky bar message on the homepage to iterate a need and a campaign goal. After several campaigns, the sticky bar can get a little stale after several uses. We want to know what would happen if we introduced a lower third gift banner that scrolls up the page (vs sitting at the top) is helpful as a NEW element to interact with during a season of highly motivated giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a lower third gift banner on the homepage during critical giving days can lead to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group experienced a 21.6% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control group. This indicates that introducing a new web element, such as a lower third ad, can capture the attention of donors and provide more gift options for interaction, ultimately boosting conversions.

Moving forward, it is important to consider the success of this experiment and use it as a basis for future experimentation. The results suggest that innovative web elements can have a positive impact on donor conversion rates, especially during crucial giving periods. Therefore, incorporating new and interactive features on the website, such as lower third banners, could potentially drive more donations in the future.

However, since the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, further testing is recommended to confirm the validity of these results. Conducting additional experiments with different variations of web elements and analyzing their impact on donor behavior could provide more insight and help fine-tune strategies for maximizing donations during campaigns.

experiments.title: Does aligning the copy on the donation page to the mission of the organization affect donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that editing the copy on the donation page to be in alignment with the mission of the organization for the recurring donor drive campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the ask to be a sustainer would match exactly what the mission of the organization is.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/07/2024
TITW ran a recurring donor drive this past summer. Since this is a very specific ask, to become a monthly donor, we wondered if aligning the copy on the donation page to the mission of TITW would increase donor conversion
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that editing the copy on the donation page to be in alignment with the mission of the organization for the recurring donor drive campaign did not lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a 24.7% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

This suggests that the initial hypothesis was not supported by the data and that the messaging alignment with the organization's mission may not have resonated with donors as expected. It is important to note that the results are not statistically significant at the 95% confidence level but are approaching validity, indicating that further testing would be necessary to confirm the findings.

experiments.title: How Adding More Than One Link In Email Affects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a second link for the FYE email will achieve a higher click rate because there will be more opportunity to click and go to the survey page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campagin every year. For this campaign the organization will publish ads, web elements, and email in an effort to support the campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase traffic to a survey page from their email channel. To do this we tested having one link vs. two link in the email body. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a second link in the FYE email resulted in a 5.2% increase in clicks for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on click rates, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

It is also important to note that once a user completes the survey, the user gets redirected to a donation page. While we don't have the data to see if increasing the number of links increased the completion rate of the survey, we can see that people who did receive the treatment had a 11% in giving at a 41% LoC (40 gifts vs 45 gifts).

experiments.title: How Adding An Image To an Email Effects Clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image for the edu FYE email appeal will achieve a higher click rate because the image will resonate with the user to continue reading the email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that consists of a full email campaign to their Education housefile.
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding an image to the email copy led to more link clicks to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we learned that adding an image to the email decreases the click rate if you look at the overall impact. To make this test even, we split the housefile into donors, nondonors, and lapsed donors. Each segment did produce a decrease in clicks, however, the treatment version with the image created a 47% lift in unsubscribes.

Looking at transactions from each email, the control also had a significant increase in transactions and revenue.

experiments.title: Does Changing the Position of an Image Affect Clicks in Email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image at the bottom of an email for the FYE EDU appeals will achieve a higher click rate because the image will be reinforcing the copy that was just read.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/25/2024
Earlier in Moody’s FYE campaign we tested adding an image towards the top of an email to see if it could catch peoples attention and make them want to read more of them email. The results showed that it did the opposite and made people click out or unsubscribe.
For this test we wanted to see if placing an image at the bottom of an email copy could reinforce the message and create a higher click rate in the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image at the bottom of an email for FYE EDU appeals did not achieve a higher click rate. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, if you look at revenue you can see a slight increase in the generosity of the donors when the image is present in the email. Both the control and treatment received the same number of donations, however the gift the average gift is up slightly when compared to the control. It is important to note that the donation size is very small, and the control did receive large outlying gifts that had to be taken out to not skew the average gift.

It would be interesting to test this tactic again to get a larger pool of donors to see if this is something that can be validated.

experiments.title: titw: Does Adding Moody Bible Institute in Emails Affect Donor Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that tying the message back to MBI for a FYE Appeal Email will achieve a higher donor conversion rate  because donors will give more if the ask is associated with the parent ministry.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/26/2024
For this appeal email, we wanted to see if a drawing a line back to Moody Bible Institute instead of Today in the Word could aspire more giving.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, which tied the message back to Moody Bible Institute instead of Today in the Word, resulted in a 24.5% decrease in donations for all traffic. Backing up the top of the funnel, there was also a 1% decrease in clicks in the treatment

experiments.title: Does a countdown clock increase urgency and donor conversion for a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a timer on the sticky bar for the fye campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a visible timeline of when the campaign ends draws more urgency than a donor countdown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/18/2024
For Moody’s FYE campaign, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion rate by replacing the donor countdown sticky bar with a different tactic to see if we could find out what their audience responds best to.
For this test we wanted to see if a timer would increase urgency more than a donor countdown
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which involved replacing the donor countdown sticky bar with a timer for the FYE campaign, resulted in a significant increase in donations for all traffic. The 38.3% increase in donations is a promising result, indicating that the timer may have indeed created a higher sense of urgency among donors.

However, the statistical level of confidence of 94.1% falls slightly short of the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This suggests that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to confirm that the timer is indeed more effective at increasing donor conversion rates compared to the donor countdown.

It's also important to note that while this did not validate with transactions. However, there was a $11,285 increase in revenue when a countdown timer was present.

experiments.title: What happens when you shorten copy on the landing page of a content offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for the People of Prayer landing page will achieve an increase in conversions because there is less friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 03/31/2024
Moody Bible Institute is a nonprofit that has multiple sectors that roll up underneath their main MBI umbrella. One of their ministries is Today in the Word, this ministries only focus is to provide daily devotionals to their audiences through magazines, email, or on the app.
We wanted to see if we could increase their email file by marketing some of the bible studies for acquisition in Facebook. The original signup rate was lower than expected. We wanted to the impact that the change in copy length would have on the conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: Shortening the copy for the People of Prayer landing page did not result in the intended increase in conversions; instead, there was a 14.5% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic. Inadvertently when shortening the copy, we could have reduced the value proposition on the page. Further testing will need to be done on the area to determine a good baseline.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency in a sticky bar impacted conversion rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visible countdown deadline for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion rate by adding a sense of urgency. To do this we added a countdown timer to a sticky bar to give a deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a visible countdown deadline to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 21.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How removing images and html styling impacted click through rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that stripping html from an email for the Today in the Word Audience will achieve a higher CTR because it will appeal more personal and less spam like.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could improve the CTR of an email by changing the layout to make it feel like it is a personal email. To do this we stripped all of the HTML, besides the header logo at the top.
In doing this it makes the email seem less like a marketing email and more like a 1 on 1 email.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that stripping HTML from an email for the Today in the Word Audience can significantly increase click-through rates (CTR). The treatment, which made the email feel more personal by removing all HTML except for the header logo, resulted in a 10.9% increase in clicks for all traffic.

experiments.title: Does having a notable figure’s presence in Spring Share content drive more donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that featuring Karl Clauson for Moody Radio Slide out visitors during Spring Share will have no impact on engagement or downstream donor metrics.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2026 - 03/02/2026
To measure the impact of featuring Moody Bible Institute President Dr. Mark Jobe’s transition from leadership, we tested whether highlighting a different staff member would affect user engagement and downstream metrics.
The test ran for approximately 2 weeks, with traffic split evenly between the 2 variants. We tracked clicks and gifts to evaluate the overall performance and impact of each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Karl Clauson's feature on Moody Radio's Spring Share slide-out yielded encouraging early signals:
Clicks: +11.1% (59.8% confidence)
 	Transactions (all traffic): +35.3% (69.7% confidence)
Both results are positive, but do not meet the 95% significance needed for firm conclusions.
Looking Ahead:
Early signs suggest Karl Clauson's presence may boost engagement and giving. More testing is needed to confirm this.
Next steps:
Running the experiment longer or expanding the sample size to build confidence
 	Review for biases or other factors that may affect the results

experiments.title: How acknowledging listeners impacts engagement with our web asset during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading with a thank-you to Radio Player listeners will increase engagement and downstream donor metrics because it acknowledges their presence, deepens their connection to Moody Radio, and invites generosity at the moment they’re already engaged.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2026 - 03/09/2026
To increase engagement on Moody Radio’s player, we tested a more direct, listener-focused headline. We believed that showing gratitude upfront would build connection and boost engagement and giving.
Thanking listeners upfront recognizes their presence, reinforces their role, and prompts generosity. This approach meets the audience while they’re already tuned in, making it a good time to invite deeper support.
This test ran in the lead-up to Spring Share. We evenly split traffic across variations to ensure a reliable comparison.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that leading with a thank-you message for Radio Player listeners did not produce a measurable lift in engagement or downstream donor metrics.
While the treatment version saw a 4.8% decrease in Content Offer CTA clicks compared to the control, the result was not statistically significant (33% confidence vs. the 95% threshold). This means we cannot reliably attribute the performance difference to the headline change.
However, looking further down the funnel provides important context. Both the control and treatment groups generated 11 gifts each. This suggests that although the thank-you message drove fewer clicks, the traffic it did generate may have been more qualified or motivated to give.
Therefore, these results should not be used to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test variations of the messaging or explore other strategies to increase engagement and donor metrics among Radio Player listeners.

experiments.title: How a donor-centric question impacts engagement on a high urgency campaign slide up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the treatment language for Moody Radio website visitors will increase clicks and downstream donor metrics because leading with a donor-focused question creates immediate emotional resonance and curiosity in the viewer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2026 - 03/14/2026
To optimize our web assets, we tested a donor-centric question in the lower third of Moody’s Spring Share campaign. The control used our standard approach; the treatment asked, “Do you love Moody Radio?”
This test builds on proven elements we’ve already implemented, adding an additional layer of optimization focused on emotional engagement.
Our hypothesis was that the treatment would drive more traffic to the donation page because the short, donor-focused question creates immediate emotional resonance and curiosity.
By inviting listeners to respond internally before fully processing the ask, the message reduces friction and increases the likelihood of a click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the donor-focused question in the treatment drove a 15.8% increase in clicks compared to the control. However, the result did not reach statistical significance (81.1% confidence vs. the 95% threshold), so it should not be used to inform definitive decisions.

More importantly, this increase in clicks did not translate into increased giving. The treatment actually saw a 5.6% decrease in gifts (22.7% confidence), suggesting that while the question sparked curiosity and drove more engagement, it did not attract users who were ready to give.

This indicates a gap between engagement and conversion. The question may have lowered friction for clicking, but it did not sufficiently reinforce motivation to take the next step.

Moving forward, it would be valuable to continue testing donor-centric language while strengthening the connection between the initial hook and the giving opportunity. Refining the message to more clearly tie emotional engagement to impact—and testing across different placements or audience segments—can help determine whether this approach can drive not just clicks, but meaningful donor action.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor goal countdown on a slide-up increase engagement and donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the dynamic donor goal in the campaign lower third for Moody Bible Institute homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream donor conversion because it shows visible progress toward a shared goal, which creates urgency and social proof—signaling that others are giving and that each gift meaningfully moves the mission forward right now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2025 - 12/31/2025
During Moody Bible Institute’s Year-End Campaign, we tested whether adding a donor goal to the website’s lower third would increase clicks and downstream donor conversion. The lower-third goal was calculated using the ministry’s overall revenue goal and average gift amount, then dynamically counted down as gifts were completed through the donation form.
The test ran for the full duration of the campaign, with traffic split evenly between the control.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic donor goal to the lower third on the Moody Bible Institute homepage showed mixed—but directionally interesting—results. While the treatment group experienced a 9.7% decrease in lower-third clicks compared to the control (42.5% level of confidence), it also saw a 5.5% increase in completed transactions (14.5% level of confidence). Although neither result reached statistical significance, the pattern suggests that the donor goal did not encourage more users to click the lower third, but it may have helped motivate those who did engage to follow through with a donation. This suggests the donor goal may be influencing donor intent and commitment rather than top-of-funnel interactions. Future testing should prioritize larger sample sizes to increase statistical confidence and yield clearer conclusions. In addition, testing alternative donor goal presentations may reveal more effective ways to communicate progress and reinforce donor motivation. While the findings are inconclusive, the directional lift in transactions suggests the donor goal has potential to strengthen conversion and merits further experimentation to better understand its impact.

experiments.title: How expanding the value proposition to reflect the impact of the entire organization—rather than a single brand—affects donor conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the “One Moody” approach for donation page visitors will not change donor conversion rates because most donors support Moody’s mission as a whole rather than a single ministry, making the broader institutional language unlikely to create friction in the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2026
To better understand the impact of consolidating the brands across Moody, we a tested more unified “One Moody” language to see how the Moody Bible Institute’s audience responded to this shift.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment suggests that implementing a more unified “One Moody” approach on the donation page was associated with a 22.6% decrease in total donations across all traffic. However, the statistical confidence level was only 69.5%, well below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%, indicating the results are not statistically reliable and should not be used to inform future decision-making. While overall donations declined in the treatment, the average gift increased. The control page generated an average gift of $20.50, while the “One Moody” treatment yielded an average gift of $23.79—an increase of approximately 16%. Because the test did not reach statistical significance, additional testing is needed before drawing conclusions about how unified “One Moody” messaging may influence donor behavior. Future experiments should prioritize achieving higher confidence levels and may benefit from further exploration of how branding and messaging influence donor perception, motivation, and giving behavior.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor goal countdown on the homepage for CYE increase engagement and donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the dynamic donor goal in the campaign lower third for Today in the Word’s Homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks as well as downstream metrics because it shows visible progress toward a shared goal, which creates urgency and social proof—signaling that others are giving and that each gift meaningfully moves the mission forward right now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2025 - 12/31/2025
During Today in the Word’s Year-End Campaign, we tested whether adding a donor goal to the website’s lower third would increase clicks and downstream donor conversion. The lower-third goal was calculated using the ministry’s overall revenue goal and average gift amount, then dynamically counted down as gifts were completed through the donation form.
The test ran for the full duration of the campaign, with traffic split evenly between the control and treatment experiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic donor goal to the lower third of the Today in the Word homepage resulted in a 4.4% increase in Lower Third slide-up CTA clicks across all traffic. However, the level of confidence for this result was 30.4%, which falls well below the standard 95% threshold, meaning the finding is not statistically valid and should not be used to inform future decisions on its own.

The treatment group also generated an 18.7% increase in completed transactions at a 55.3% level of confidence. While this result does not validate a definitive lift in clicks or gifts, it is directionally positive and suggests that the presence of a donor goal does not demotivate the Today in the Word audience—and may help reinforce giving behavior among engaged users.

Future experimentation should focus on increasing sample size and extending test duration to improve statistical confidence and account for normal fluctuations in donor behavior.

Overall, while these results are not conclusive, the positive directional trends indicate that further testing is warranted to better understand whether a donor goal in the homepage lower third can meaningfully drive engagement and downstream donor conversion..

experiments.title: How including an image of the premium in a web asset donation ask impacts engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the premium image for Moody Radio website radio player visitors will achieve an increase in engagement and downstream donor metrics because  it visually reinforces the offer and increasing its perceived value to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/03/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding a premium image to the website Radio Player.
Both the control and treatment included identical premium language; however, only the treatment featured the premium image.
The test ran for two weeks, with traffic evenly split between both variants.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment showed that adding an image to the Moody Radio Player led to a 20.6% decrease in clicks overall (77.1% level of confidence). Because this outcome was unexpected, we took a deeper look at the data to better understand what may have driven the decline.
To isolate potential drivers, we analyzed click performance by device type.
On mobile, the treatment resulted in a 12.7% decrease in clicks (48% level of confidence).
On desktop, clicks declined by 44.8% (72% level of confidence).
On tablet, clicks dropped by 52.7% (62% level of confidence).
These results suggest that the image had the greatest negative impact on desktop and tablet users, where the decline in engagement was most pronounced.
Transaction data followed a similar pattern. The control generated four gifts, while the treatment generated just one—representing a 75% decrease in transactions (82% level of confidence).

experiments.title: How a Percentage-Based Goal Thermometer Drives Donor Conversion in High-Urgency Campaigns
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a percentage-based goal thermometer to the CYE Giving page will increase donor conversion. By showing progress without emphasizing the total dollar amount, the thermometer helps donors see their gift as a meaningful contribution to a shared goal, motivating them to join in and take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s Year-End campaign, we tested adding a percentage-based goal thermometer. Previous tests with a dollar-based thermometer on giving pages showed less impact on donation lift than expected.
We hypothesized that this was due to the size of the overall goal—individual donors may have felt that their gift wouldn’t make a meaningful impact on progress.
This approach aimed to increase donor engagement, and early results indicated it was successful.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is clear. Adding a percentage-based goal thermometer to giving pages increased donations by 20.8% for all traffic. This indicates that displaying progress as a percentage can enhance donor conversion rates.
However, the statistical confidence level was 75.3%, below the 95% threshold. This means the results may not be reliable for future decisions, though they are still directional. Future studies should further investigate the impact of the percentage-based thermometer, possibly by modifying its design or placement. It is also important to ensure that experiments are well-powered and have high statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: Does emphasizing collective impact strengthen a donor’s sense of shared mission, or does it unintentionally reduce their sense of personal responsibility to give?
experiments.researchQuestion: Including collective impact language in the Fall Share Email will help recipients achieve an increase in engagement and downstream conversion, as donors will be invited to see their gift as part of a larger, shared mission.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2025
In an effort to better understand what motivates Moody Radio’s audience to engage with an email and ultimately give, we tested how emphasizing collective impact influences donor behavior. Specifically, we wanted to see whether highlighting the power of “together” encourages greater involvement—or if it unintentionally reduces a donor’s sense of personal responsibility to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that using collective impact language in Fall Share Email 5 led to a 15.5% increase in click engagement across all traffic. However, the results reached only a 65.0% confidence level, which falls below the standard 95% threshold for statistical significance. As such, these findings should be viewed as directional rather than conclusive.

When examining donor conversion, the results were mixed:
Control: 10 transactions, $462 in revenue
 	Treatment: 7 transactions, $905 in revenue
While the treatment generated
fewer gifts overall
, it produced a
higher average gift size
, suggesting that collective impact framing may increase perceived value or motivation among those who choose to give.
Moving forward, continued testing is recommended to validate these initial trends. Future experiments could include
larger sample sizes
,
alternative expressions of collective impact
, or
different audience segments
to understand better how this message framing influences both engagement and giving behavior. Additionally, evaluating the
context and tone
in which collective impact language appears may help determine how best to align this approach with Moody Radio’s audience motivations and communication strategy.

experiments.title: Does impact-focused or campaign-focused language lead to more engagement and an increase in downstream metrics?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the impact-focused language for Moody Radio radio player visitors will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the donors to connect emotionally to the impact of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2025 - 08/07/2025
For Moody Radio’s 48 Hours of Impact campaign, we wanted to test whether campaign-focused copy or impact-focused copy resonated more with donors. Moody has historically been very campaign-focused with their appeals, since their audience is most likely accustomed to this type of appeal. We wanted to know whether the additional value of their gift would be more impactful than traditional language.
Control: Campaign-focused
Treatment: Impact-focused
This test ran on the Moody Radio radio player for about two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact-focused language did not perform better than the campaign-focused language in terms of generating clicks for Moody Radio's 48 Hours of Impact campaign. The treatment actually saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.9%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

Furthermore, we used custom metrics in VWO to track gifts that came from the Radio Player.

experiments.title: How eliminating the form frame and simplifying opt-in language on acquisition landing pages impacts email opt ins
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that eliminating the form frame and simplifying the opt-in copy for Moody Radio acquisition landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email optins because it reduces the friction and decision fatigue in the form submission process allowing for a more streamlined approach for the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 08/17/2025
In an effort to increase email opt-ins on Moody Radio’s acquisition funnels, we wanted to try simplifying the messaging around opting in as well as cleaning up the form around it.
The treatment removed the form frame as well as condensed down the language around the checkbox to opt in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which simplified the opt-in copy and removed the form frame for Moody Radio's acquisition landing page, did not lead to an increase in email opt-ins compared to the control. The treatment actually resulted in a 5.0% decrease in Opt-in checkbox clicked for All Traffic with a statistical level of confidence of 60.2%. Therefore, the hypothesis that reducing friction and decision fatigue would streamline the process for users and increase opt-ins was not supported by the data.

On the positive side, the experiment did show a significant increase in Revenue by 216% with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that while the simplified messaging did not lead to more opt-ins, it may have had a positive impact on revenue generation.

Moving forward, the insights gained from this experiment suggest that when making changes to acquisition landing pages, it is essential to carefully consider the potential impact on user behavior and to thoroughly test any hypothesis before implementing changes. Additionally, when interpreting results, it is important to focus on statistical significance levels of at least 95% to ensure the validity of the findings. Finally, while an increase in revenue is positive, it should be balanced with other key metrics such as opt-in rates to fully evaluate the effectiveness of a new approach.

experiments.title: How an autoresponder sent from a person versus the organization impacts clicks

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the autoresponder sent from a notable person within the organization resulted in a 2.1% decrease in clicks compared to the autoresponder sent from the organization directly. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 25.9%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not conclusive and should not be used for future decision-making.

While we know that opens are no longer reliable, we did pull the results of both opens and click-to-open rates to understand the range of where each email performed.

Control: 65.48% open rate, 64.93% click to open rate

Treatment: 61.23% open rate, 67.96% click to open rate

These results suggest that the treatment email, coming from Wes Ward, resulted in a slightly lower open rate of the email, but led to a slightly higher click-to-open rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide statistically significant results. Additionally, considering factors such as the timing of the emails, the messaging, and the audience segmentation could help to identify what truly drives engagement and clicks. It is also important to test different variables in future experiments to gain a better understanding of what resonates with the audience and drives the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio did not lead to an increase in clicks, as initially hypothesized. In fact, we observed a 4.2% decrease in clicks across all traffic, with a statistical confidence of 36.3%. This is well below the accepted 95% threshold for validity.

Additionally, downstream metrics showed a 4.4% decrease in transactions, with a confidence level of only 13.3%.

These findings suggest that the gift array version of the footer did not effectively drive more users to the donation page and may have resulted in less motivated traffic. While these metrics did not meet the validity criteria, they point to the possibility that there may be more effective ways to encourage donations from Moody Radio listeners beyond the gift array format.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that what may seem like a logical approach to increasing user engagement, such as allowing users to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page, may not always lead to the desired outcome. It is essential to test all changes thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically sound and reach a high level of confidence before drawing conclusions.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that the addition of a goal thermometer on the FYE donation page led to a 2.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence at 19.1%. This means that while there was a positive impact, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

However, revenue saw a substantial 400% decrease with 100% confidence, indicating a clear negative impact from the goal thermometer. These results suggest that Moody Bible’s audience may be more inclined to make smaller donations when a goal thermometer is present, potentially due to the high fundraising target set for the FYE campaign. Further testing is needed to refine these insights and draw more definitive conclusions. It may also be worth exploring the use of a daily campaign goal, which could help reduce the perceived pressure of reaching a large target and encourage more frequent contributions.

Moving forward, it is important to take into consideration the statistical significance of the results when evaluating the effectiveness of different strategies. While the idea of leveraging social proof through a goal thermometer showed some promise in increasing donor conversion metrics, further testing and optimization are necessary to draw valid conclusions. It is important to refine the messaging and design of the goal thermometer to ensure it effectively communicates progress towards a communal objective without negatively impacting revenue. Future experimentation should focus on testing variations of the goal thermometer to find the most effective approach for driving donor conversions without compromising financial outcomes.

Moody Radio hosts a Spring Share Fundraiser each year where a ton of traffic is driven tot he website from on-air, paid ads, and social.

These results should not be used for future decision making. It is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to determine the true impact of adding a personal ask to the dear reader.
