However, similar to the Radio test we did see a massive increase in revenue from the dynamic sticky bar. In fact, we saw the same $4k lift in revenue. We're still digging into outlier gifts with the moody team but, it is interesting that this test is following suit.

experiments.title: Will a Dynamic sticky bar work better than a wiki for Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage sticky bar element will achieve a higher donor conversion because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 we wanted to test a different approach to have on the homepage for Giving Tuesday, which in turn would inform the control that we would need to have for the next test.
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar with a rotating message would increase donor conversion rather than having a wiki bar at the top of the page. This test would only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment with the rotating message in the sticky bar did not perform better than the control with a static wiki in terms of donor conversion. Despite seeing a 4.9% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 22.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

However, while this test did not validate on transactions, it has a significant increase in revenue. In fact, the revenue for the treatment was $4,200 more than the control. Based on the revenue being significantly higher we went ahead and rolled out the dynamic sticky bar to be our control for the next test. We are working with the Moody team to see if there was a major gift included in the results to make the jump in revenue that drastic.

experiments.title: Is a specific ask better than an open ended ask?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific gift amount for a slide in ask on the internal pages of the website will achieve a higher donor conversion because having a suggested gift amount will decrease decision friction.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
During previous campaigns we have found that putting a slide in ask on the internal pages of the Moody Bible Institute (edu) website increases donations to the campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if we could increase the average gift, while also not sacrificing donations, by asking for a specific gift amount of $60. To get to the $60 ask we looked at their overall average gift (excluding majors). Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a specific gift amount suggested in a slide in ask on the internal pages of the Moody Bible Institute website led to a significant decrease in donations. While the hypothesis suggested that providing a suggested amount would reduce decision friction and increase donor conversion, the results showed the opposite effect.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the potential impact of suggesting a specific gift amount on donor behavior before implementing any changes. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that statistically significant results are achieved with a high level of confidence before making any decisions based on the outcomes of an experiment.

It is also recommended to consider conducting further tests or adjusting the approach to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to potentially uncover the most effective strategy for increasing donations without sacrificing donor conversion.

experiments.title: How testimonials on the donation page increased donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor testimonial for the main campaign donation page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because they can clearly see the impact of their gift during CYE.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2024 - 12/16/2024
During the high-traffic month of December, we wanted to know if adding an “end user” testimonial would further inspire potential donors to give or give at a greater amount because they were reading about the impact of their gift at the time of decision. Similar to how an e-commerce page might use testimonials for a product, we wanted to know the impact of adding this additional copy from the end user to the Today in the Word CYE donation page would make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor testimonial to the main campaign donation page resulted in a 17.3% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that having end-user testimonials on the page can increase credibility and potentially encourage more people to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of donor testimonials or other forms of social proof on donation pages. By conducting more experiments with larger sample sizes and ensuring a higher level of statistical confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform our strategies for optimizing donation pages and increasing donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Does Having a Dynamic Text Sticky Bar Increase Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having dynamic text for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the dynamic text will decrease banner blindness and present more than one value proposition to become a donor.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/24/2024
We have previously tested into the use of sticky bars during high-urgency campaigns for Moody.
For this campaign, we wondered if changing the text to be dynamic instead of static we could catch the eye of more users and ultimately lead them to convert on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed a 19.7% increase in conversion at a 54% level of confidence with the first treatment of the dynamic texting. This suggests that our approach to incorporating dynamic text can be effective, especially when the copy is tailored to focus on the donor's perspective rather than the organization's. Conversely, the second treatment resulted in a 9% decrease in conversion, reinforcing the importance of donor-centric messaging.

From this, we can infer that dynamic text has the potential to significantly boost conversion rates when crafted thoughtfully. Specifically, emphasizing the donor's role, impact, or benefits appears to engage the audience more effectively than messaging that centers around the organization itself. However, to fully understand the scope and limitations of this tactic, it will be necessary to conduct additional tests across different placements and contexts. This further research will help us identify where and how dynamic text performs optimally, allowing us to refine our strategies and maximize conversions in various scenarios.

experiments.title: Will Adding “Moody Bible Institute” in an Appeal Increase Transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding Moody Bible Institute for a Fall Share appeal email will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because adding in Moody Bible Institute adds even more credibility to the organization and gives potential donors the chance to associate their gift with something bigger.
experiments.summary:

Moody Radio runs a Fall Share each year and it reaches a lot of people on air and online.
For this test, we wanted to see if associating Moody Radio with Moody Bible Institute in an email will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding "Moody Bible Institute" to a Fall Share appeal email for Moody Radio resulted in a 10.8% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 18.6%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for validity. As a result, these findings should not be used for future decision making.

It is important to note that this experiment did not validate for our original hypothesis, however we did see a directional increase in revenue. The treatment saw a 127% lift in revenue at a 85% LoC.

experiments.title: Rad: Does a raw link or a button create more clicks?

For this test we wanted to see if the moody radio subscribers prefer a button in an email or a raw link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a raw link in an email campaign resulted in a 9.0% increase in donations compared to using a button. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 45.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different elements of the email campaign to determine what resonates best with Moody Radio subscribers.

experiments.title: Can a Donor Countdown Be More Effective than a Static Sticky Bar?

experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor countdown to the sticky bar on the home page did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. This suggests that in this particular case, the dynamic element did not have the intended effect of making the sticky bar more appealing to users.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of adding dynamic elements to key areas of the website. It may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different types of dynamic content or in different placements to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that any findings are statistically significant and have a high level of confidence before making decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Does Having Dynamic Text Sticky Bar Have the Same Affect Post On-Air?

For this test, we wanted to see if these same results held true during the post-on-air portion of the campaign. Since we already know donor-centric language is better, we tested only that treatment against the static sticky bar. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that donor-centric language in the form of a dynamic text sticky bar for the homepage during the post-on-air campaign can lead to an 18.2% increase in donations compared to a static sticky bar. This confirms the earlier finding that donor-centric language is more effective in prompting conversions. However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment is below the recommended threshold of 95%, which means the results may not be entirely reliable for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we should continue to focus on using donor-centric language in our messaging and explore different ways to incorporate this into our campaigns. It's also important to ensure that our experiments have a high level of statistical confidence to draw more definitive conclusions. Additionally, we should consider testing other elements of the sticky bar, such as design and placement, to further optimize its effectiveness in driving donations.

While this test did produce a decrease in conversions, we actually saw donors give more generously when the sticky bar is present on the page. We saw a $354 average gift with the treatment and a $293 average gift on the control.

experiments.title: Does having a specific ask help increase donor conversion on a dear reader?

experiments.title: Will adding a sticky bar to a campaign donation page affect donor conversion?

Moving forward, it is important to recognize that although the treatment showed a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the lack of high statistical confidence means that these results should not be used for future decision making at this time. It would be beneficial to conduct additional experiments with larger sample sizes or different variations to further investigate the impact of presenting a specific gift amount.

In future experimentation, it would be wise to continue testing variations of presenting specific gift amounts to donors to see if it consistently leads to increased conversion rates. experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dear reader for high traffic pages will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the likelihood to donate when presented with with an opportunity after receiving content that is of high value is easier.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2024 - 04/10/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion by adding an easy conversion opportunity right on the page.
We ran this experiment with Today in the Word, a daily devotional ministry. We only targeted the pages that contained the daily devotionals. These pages get a ton of traffic each day.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Adding a donation opportunity directly on high traffic pages can lead to an increase in donor conversion rates.

2. The treatment group saw a significant 12.1% increase in transactions for all traffic.

3. The statistical level of confidence was only 55.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85% for validity.

4. These results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

For future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is at least 85% in order to draw reliable conclusions. It may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding donation opportunities on high traffic pages with a larger sample size or different variations to see if the results can be replicated and validated. It is also important to consider other factors that may have influenced the results, such as the timing of the experiment or any external events that may have impacted donor behavior.

The Today in the Word Daily Devotion Pages receive a ton of traffic daily. We wanted to see if we could get some of this traffic to convert into being a donor by presenting a ask at the end of the devotion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By presenting an ask at the end of the content we were able to see a 364% lift in conversion at a 100% level of confidence.
This experiment was a test of nothing vs. something (adding an ask), further testing can be done to see if we can increase the donor conversion or average gift by presenting the ask differently.

experiments.title: How 1 call-to-action vs 2 calls-to-action in an email impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of CTA for an email will achieve a higher click rate because the opportunity to click has grown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of calls-to-action (CTA) in an email can potentially lead to a higher click rate. In this case, the treatment with two CTAs resulted in an 18.5% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control group with only one CTA. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.5%, which means that these results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical significance of the results. It may also be beneficial to explore the impact of different types of CTAs (such as buttons vs. hyperlinks) and the effect of using HTML vs. plain text emails. By continuing to experiment and test different variables, we can gather more reliable data to make informed decisions about optimizing email performance for Moody Radio.

experiments.title: How having multiple calls-to-action impacts the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the number of ctas in an email for the mass communication emails will achieve a higher percentage of engagement because there will be more opportunities throughout the email to engage with the content.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2024
This is the last test in our design of experiments to make a mass marketing email feel like a personal 1-on-1 email.
We started testing with HTML vs. Plain text and then pivoted to how we present CTA’s within the email (button or raw URL). This final test will be seeing how many cta’s we should put in the email itself. Will the Audience respond better to only having one or is it more ideal to have two?
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that increasing the number of CTAs in an email can lead to a higher percentage of engagement, as evidenced by a 6.9% increase in clicks for all traffic in the treatment group. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 20.4%, which is below the typically accepted threshold of 85%. This experiment will need to be retested with a plain text email.
